speaker: interviewer
text: This is Edith Abrahams, tape two, October 18, 1995. Edith, we were discussing an incident at the Romer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Romer is important to me because I spent some time there. My sister, who's just two years older than I am, Margot and I, we used to go and just investigate because it was the old part of Frankfurt. It was an interesting part of Frankfurt, because the Romer itself was the original city hall, and they used to put on plays with playwrights, like authors, like Shakespeare. But it wasn't Shakespeare, it was Goethe and Schiller, which were its major German authors.

speaker: interviewer
text: So to me, it was quite a shock when books were burned there. I also remember in-- just before we moved from Chernivitsi that a blimp was a major event for us. We went to the roof of the apartment building and we watched this blimp called the Graf Zeppelin. It was going overhead very, very slowly. And we thought this would be future aviation.

speaker: interviewee
text: This very same Graf Zeppelin, this blimp, came down in New Jersey many, many years later. I don't know how many years later, but it came down. And that took care of that particular aviation. And these were all important events in my life. But let's get back to-- I just want to tell you about active boycott. We had an incident there in that apartment in [INAUDIBLE] in Sachsenhausen. We had really beautiful artwork. My father had bought a painting which was the size of a-- almost like a mirror. It was a very big painting. And he also was a collector of fine art that came from the Boxer Wars, from the-- the Chinese, Japanese Boxer Wars. There were two main Chinese vases. They were life, good-sized vases. And they were valuable. I remember my dad discussing the fact that it had this crack on, which was, he said, it's caused by the glazes. So we know they were valuable. He also had a little ivory paintbrush holder, which he got from that time. It's the only thing that I have that came out of there. I have it here. And I'll show it to you later. But the vases and also a samovar, which is a sort of a brass pot with these lions. They're very valuable. And a painting. Now, those were visible. Those three things were visible-- or four things-- two vase and that. And apparently, someone must have told someone, some of the Nazis that we had this valuable artwork. Because they came to us in our apartment, and they said, you're donating that. We are going to take that. Actually, what they did is they just took it. And we said, yeah, sure. And that was the end of it. We never saw it again. These-- these were really our valuables. But the time came that my dad had to leave Germany and we knew we'd have to leave, too. And at the time, I don't remember, you know, it was stressful, and I don't remember exactly what we did with the store. He somehow dissolved it. I'm sure he didn't sell it. But he did slowly, after April of 1933, began to take some money out of the bank. He had some accounts. Not everything, but he took as much as he could slowly. He then had a friend in a nearby town, which was called Offenbach, which was also involved in the leather good business. And there was a-- one of our friends who was Jewish, who was a repairman, and he took a briefcase. He took the briefcase, which was new, and made it look old. He separated the lining. He actually opened the lining-- I don't know exactly how. But he pasted money inside there. There was newspaper that was in there, also. I-- I have no idea how a briefcase is lined. But it took quite a while to do this, to make it look old. They put acid to make the-- the metal look older. And he glued it back in such a way that you could not tell it had ever been disturbed. And then he decided he had to go. We didn't have-- we-- in order to leave Germany at that point, you didn't need a visa. I don't know how or-- or whatever it was. It was not like coming in the United States. And he decided he was leaving. It was extremely stressful, and I have pictures to show you from the day that my dad left. It was very hard. Anyway, my dad left to go and our signal was that he was going to Strasbourg and he was going to call us. And we were very, very frightened because just two days before we left-- he left, a man, a friend of our family-- I don't know who he was-- also left Germany, and he had hidden some money in a bird cage. It was like a double floor. He had been taken off the train and we knew we never heard from this man again. So I don't know what happened to him and we were very frightened. My dad, I remember, he took a tranquilizer with him before he went on the train. And with a heavy heart, we said goodbye to him. The-- the plan was, he was going to Strasbourg. And in Strasbourg, he was going to call us. And the signal was that he would call and say to my mother happy birthday, and he was sorry that he could not get a cake for her in time for her birthday. The interesting thing was he left on August 13, and her birthday was actually 10 days later, August 23rd. So this was very reasonable for us. The not being able to get the cake would mean that he got the money through. And that was the signal. We waited with a heavy heart, until we stood near-- we stayed near that telephone, and when he hit Strasbourg, and he was across the border, he called us. And then we knew. We had packed already. We were leaving the next day. The family saw us off at the train station. We took with us really our-- just our belongings because we didn't know, you know, like clothing, that's about it. Because we didn't know where we were going to be. We went to Strasbourg and we stayed there for about a week and we played tourists. My dad was a very unusual kind of man, made the decision where we were going to go from there. It was-- it was really very unusual. I remember that day, we were in the hotel, he spread out the map. He looked at the map, and he said, well, let's not go to Paris, which would have been logical. Most refugees would go to Paris. And for this particular reason, he felt we shouldn't go there because we wouldn't be able to make a living. There were so many refugees. They were overrunning the city. And he said, well, let's not go up to Bordeaux, because that-- there's nothing commercial there. That's where the wines are made, and farms, and so on. Let's not go to Marseille. Marseille was a harbor city. He was well-acquainted with harbor cities because [INAUDIBLE] had been a harbor city. And Hamburg, when his family was from that area, he said no. He said there's too much crime and I wouldn't trust you going to school there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he picked Lyon. It's a dot on the map, and he said, well, this seems to be located pretty well. Grenoble, I heard, there's a university there, and let's go to Grenoble. And that's how we went. We don't know a soul. And he even made it appear like fun. And that's how we got to Lyon. Now Lyon was-- it was a commercial city and we were there exactly-- let's see-- eight months. And during that eight months, my dad tried very hard to really try to make a living. And it didn't work. He tried actually three different businesses. I don't know. Are you interested in hearing this? Certainly.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. The first business he decided he would-- he had heard that there were a lot of working people, and he decided that there are people needed-- like you would have jeans here, their people had like coveralls. And he had it all planned. He was going to be, not the manufacturer, but the middleman. He would farm out the-- the-- the manufacturing of these coveralls because he discovered that there were women who were sitting in their house with sewing machines, and he had it all-- he figured there wouldn't be any problem. He would get people to work for him. He kept the patterns and what he wanted. He even had some leads on who to sell it to. The actual transactions were done through my sister. My sister Renata was a linguist and she spoke French beautifully. We had learned a little bit of French in school, at the Philanthropin, but not much. But my sister was pretty well versed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she got the leads. As far as the women were concerned, he had advertised, and there were women that were willing to-- to do this. But, unfortunately, the French are not that industrious. The deadline that my father was used to, being in the insurance business, never existed. And the whole thing came apart before it even started.

speaker: interviewee
text: The second business that he tried was-- I don't know where he got the idea-- but it was wholesale grains. He rented a store. It was actually like a little empty something. It was not necessarily in a-- in an area where the-- where merchants were. It was just cheap. And he ordered barrels of different grains. We had lentils, and peas, and you name it. He was able to put some of those into sacks, and he tried to then reach the merchants to deal with them. And it fell apart because he couldn't really deliver it. He had-- I don't know why he had picked that kind of business, but it also came apart before it began. During that period, he was very concerned what other refugees were doing. And they, apparently, had a grapevine going. And, you know, we didn't really discuss these things over the dining room table. My dad handled it, and we trusted him. There was a-- a very good friend that we had met. He was an attorney. He was dealing with a lot of different refugees. And he suggested that my father should invest in certain things because none of the businesses were really working out. Unfortunately, we never realized that this man who my father trusted was a gambler. And the sad thing about that was that he-- my dad had invested much more than we could afford, and the man apparently gambled away, and then he committed suicide. And here we were in France, with no business, with no livelihood. My sister Margot and I had been in school, and I had become pretty fluent in French, but we saw no way that we could make a living there. We also found there was a resistance against the Germans. That's something we hadn't counted on. Even though we were Jewish, the French had still carried a grudge over us for so many years about the Germans. And we were many times we were called [INAUDIBLE]. So my dad really felt we couldn't stay there. As far as the family in the United States, for some reason, my dad never really contacted them at that time. He was in touch with his private secretary we-- we were close friends with, from-- originally from the [INAUDIBLE]. She had taken her mother to Holland, and we were in touch with her. And she felt, with their correspondence, that we should move to Holland. And without hesitation, my dad decided that's what we're gonna do. I remember my mother once, just before we left for Holland, she-- excuse me-- went to see my grandparents, went back to Germany, and we were very worried about that. She was there I think for about a week, because my grandmother had become ill. And as soon as my mother returned, we went on to Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: She did not have any trouble getting in or out of Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that time. I-- you know, I can't really tell you details. I remember we worried a great deal. I don't know how she was able to do that and come back again, but there seemed to be no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1934. As a matter of fact, it was the spring of 1934, I remember that now. And-- but we left, I think it was April that we went to Amsterdam. In fact, it was interesting, when we got to the station in Amsterdam, when we got off the train after we had made that whole trip to-- through Paris and all that, not seeing any sites, just going because we had a destination. We got off the train in-- in Amsterdam and a baggage carrier came and he said shalom to us. And I couldn't believe it. I had never heard of a Jewish baggage carrier, and someone who said shalom, and it was a nice welcome for us. But the welcome that we were getting in Amsterdam we kind of didn't expect. Because, whenever you go from one country to another, if you don't have a visa, you immediately have to appear at immigration.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we didn't have a visa. We went into immigration and they told us-- they asked us where we were coming from. And we-- we said from France. And they said, well, you're tourists. And we said no, no, we're not tourists. We fled Germany. They said that's a technicality we don't accept. If you had come in from Germany directly, you would then be considered immigrants. But you're not. Because you were coming from France and you are tourists, you have to post 15-- I think it was 1,500 guilder. It was an enormous amount of money.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if it was per person. I just remember the sum of 1,500 guilder. They announced to us that we could stay for one month and then make sure that we have papers to go elsewhere because we couldn't stay. And where to go, they said, well, you'll have to go back to Germany then. They said they might-- father was extremely upset because he had no idea what he was going to do-- so they explained that we might get a grace period of another month, but that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the meanwhile, they gave us the first month, and we settled into an apartment in Amsterdam. And then my father could not get a work permit. There was no-- no way we could make a living. So the three of us girls went to work. And my older sister went-- this is very interesting-- she worked for the Frank family, you know? Anne Frank, and-- with that family when they were in the grain business. I mean, it was long before we ever knew about that story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Otto Frank had hired my sister, and she worked in their household. My middle sister, Margot, worked for another family. I have no idea who they were. I worked for a physician. Her name was Dr. [? Riesenfeld, ?] who had also come from Germany but had settled in Holland. She really didn't need me. I was only 14 years old, and she didn't need a kid in the house. And she did it because she felt she could help us. She knew we needed money. She had a housekeeper already, and all I did really was keep the housekeeper company and she taught me to do some tests that she was doing in her laboratory. But there was really no reason for me to be there except her kindness. After a month, we decided we would hang on a little longer, and we asked them for an extension, and they gave us the extension. They said-- they wrote-- they said we were actually deported, but we would have that month. The reason that we decided that we would take just the month because we-- at that time, we had already decided we would live there illegally because other refugees were doing the exact same thing. We moved to a town called Diemen, and it was on the outskirts of Amsterdam. And it was being run by a mayor and he was corrupt. And he was taking money on the side, and he didn't ask any questions. And that's all we cared about. We found a little attic apartment in Diemen. And I continued my work, and so did my sisters, and we traveled. I was really beginning to love Amsterdam. It had never occurred to me it would end at all. I would spend Sundays at the Rijksmuseum because I loved art. Art was always my love. I'd read about Rembrandt and other artists that I liked. And as far as-- I mean, we had made friends. And as far as I'm concerned, I was going to be Dutch.

speaker: interviewer
text: The only hitch was the time was going out, and we were staying there much longer. It was the month went by, and two months, and much more time. And it was getting to be October and I was a very skinny kid. And I was always cold. And I was bugging my father for my winter coat because they were in storage. We had a box with our belongings which had been shipped to us from Germany, originally to France and-- which included our heavy clothing. Because at the beginning, we didn't need them. And my father was very-- Pappy, I called him-- he-- Pappy was so annoyed with me. Why do I need the clothes now? But I persisted, and he finally said it was all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to this storage facility and we unpacked it, and it really literally saved our lives. Because we discovered that we had purchased-- we had subscribed to the liberal newspapers in France who had all the information what has been going on in Germany during the period we were away, during Nazism. And there were atrocities, and they were all available in the rest of the countries, but not in Germany because Germany was a dictatorship. But the news were leaking out to France. There was a-- a book and a newspaper we had. The-- I think the book was called which means the torch. And it got photos of atrocities of people had come out of camps, or-- or-- we didn't even know about camps at that time. Or had been beaten and so on in there. These were substantiated things that were in this particular book, or in the papers. We immediately hired a couple people to repack everything. This took hours. And when we got all finished, and I had my winter coat, my dad and I, we hugged each other. Because if we had gone back, we knew eventually, at that point we realized, we would have to go back to Germany. If we had gone back with that information in those boxes, they would have immediately killed us. And would-- there was no doubt about it. And it wasn't much after that, I think it was two weeks after I got my winter coat. It was very cold already in Amsterdam. The wind was blowing, and my mother and I were downtown. We had been shopping in a department store. And when we come rounding a corner, we ran smack into the immigration officer that had actually deported us. He pointed his finger and my mother and I, we began to ran-- to run. But we knew we'd be found. There's no question about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had no choice but to go back to Germany. At that point, it was in 19-- let's see, that was the end of 19-- it was October 1934. I think was the end of 1934. We then began to send telegrams and letters to the USA, and we really didn't get response. So I don't think I'll ever forget the day that we went across that border. Because we were on the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like-- it's almost like if you-- I visualize a person who might be-- have been in a burning house, they will always have the smell of that something that's been singed. That's how I felt when we got on that train. I had not looked at any brown shirt stormtroopers all those-- this whole time, and I had sort of taken it out of my memory. And there they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we got across the border, the SS-- not the SS, not the black-shirted, the SR, the brown ones, got on the train. You had to show your papers. And they asked us where are you going? And we said we're going to Frankfurt. They advised us that the next morning that my dad-- my father had to go to the Gestapo to-- to tell-- to-- the way-- to get papers, et cetera, et cetera. We stayed with my aunt and uncle in Frankfurt. And the next morning after we arrived, my dad did go to the Gestapo. Then he came back and told us about that particular incident. On the-- on the desk of the-- this was not the SR, it was the SS, the black-shirted. He had this big ledgers, and across the ledgers, it said Juden-- Jews. He opened it to the initial W-- our name was Wolf-- and he said, where were you? My father said, we had been in France and in Holland. He said, what did you do in France? My said-- you know, where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father started to tell him. And the strangest thing happened. He looked down in the book. He started filling in the things that my father was just going to tell him. Pappy was-- was beginning and he was literally finishing the sentences. And that was-- we thought that was very strange. And then we decided, when Pappy was telling us when he came home that they might have kept track of people that had been prominent before. And we were-- felt very lucky that we were not politically involved when we had lived in France and in Holland. Because if we had, things might have been a great deal different. When we first came back to Frankfurt, of course, we had nowhere to stay. We had no livelihood. But through our luck, there was a club, a Jewish club, which was rare, but it was still-- it was still permitted at that time. It was in a building called [GERMAN], which means a circle of friends, or something like that. I can't translate it exactly. And this was actually a men's club, a Jewish men's club. The men would come and play cards and spend the day. And the building was owned by a Jewish organization. They needed a caterer, and since my mother had come from the hotel industry and was very familiar with that sort of thing, we drifted into it without any problem. So my dad was the manager. My mother did the cooking. We actually did not cook dinners. It was just lunch and afternoon. In Germany, everybody is into snacks, and into coffee, and desserts, and that kind of thing, and-- but big lunches. The three of us, we were the waitresses. I should have been in school then-- by then, because I was getting to be 15 years old. But my parents needed us. And I did not go to school. Neither did my sister Margot. This went on for a while, until the Nazis decided that the Jews did not need to socialize-- whether it was men or women, didn't make any difference. And they just closed the building.

speaker: interviewer
text: My father, who had a lot of audacity, decided at that time that if this was successful, why not open a Jewish restaurant? We discussed the merits, and we thought it was a good idea, but we didn't like what he had in mind. First place, he picked a location, which was very audacious. It was not an off street. It was right in the downtown section right near the [INAUDIBLE]. And it was an empty apartment, I think, above some stores. There was a sign along-- in-- inside of the-- the vestibule, there was a sign that said [GERMAN], which means restaurant [INAUDIBLE]. They had a Magen David. Not only that, he had a very large-- he had decided on a very large white sign, a glass sign-- you know, one of those block things-- and it will-- should have a Magen David, you know, the Star of David in it, which was audacious. And we thought he was crazy. We-- you know, why advertise something like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he felt, well, the Jewish community needs a restaurant. We're here and we're going to give it to them. And we said, they're going to break the sign. They're going to come [INAUDIBLE]. And my father said, I'll handle it when it comes. So he opened this restaurant, and again, we were the waitresses. He did even more. He hired two-- well, this time, my mother was not the cook. She just sort of supervised. We had a woman-- a man who was a-- a cook, who was Jewish, a woman who was his helper, who was Jewish, one waitress, and then there were two other waitresses who were not Jewish. It was interesting.
